title: Native systemd socket support for quadlet
body:
### Feature request description

I want to socket activate a container (caddy) so that I don't have much system overhead if the container is not used.

### Suggest potential solution

Currently I think we need to manually create a .socket file for systemd in order for systemd to activate the quadlet service. It would be nice if quadlet natively support a `[Socket]` section and generate the systemd socket file.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

I tried to manually create a systemd socket file. Just as quadlet systemd service generator is a life quality improvement. I hope that we can have quadlet systemd socket generator.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
> Currently I think we need to manually create a .socket file for systemd in order for systemd to activate the quadlet service. It would be nice if quadlet natively support a [Socket] section and generate the systemd socket file.

So basically you like [Socket] section in addition to [Container]? And then quadlet creates two units, the service and socket files from that?

I don't mind the suggestion but it does sort of break the one to one relationship that one quadlet is one systemd unit and I am not sure if this will cause other problems for us later on.

Is one extra file really that bad? I mean that is simply how systemd services are supposed to work? I guess one point of friction is that the container file is one one directory while the .socket files would be in another (i.e. the regular systemd directory).
One thing I was thinking here as something like timers have the same problem if would make sense if we could have regular units that we just copy in the quadlet directories as well. Would that help?

cc @ygalblum

> So basically you like [Socket] section in addition to [Container]? And then quadlet creates two units, the service and socket files from that?


Exactly. I want quadlet to manage these two units for me. I was also thinking about *.timer as well. For me it would be great if we can just copy other types of systemd units. In my opinion, quadlet has done a lot to reduce the fiction of managing container using systemd. I don't really want to go back to managing *.socket or *.timer with systemd itself again.

> Exactly. I want quadlet to manage these two units for me. I was also thinking about *.timer as well. For me it would be great if we can just copy other types of systemd units. In my opinion, quadlet has done a lot to reduce the fiction of managing container using systemd. I don't really want to go back to managing *.socket or *.timer with systemd itself again.

That's exactly the downside of this ask as it makes Quadlet sort of a generic systemd wrapper. Something it never meant to (or should) be. We start with `.socket`, continue with `.timer` and what comes next? 

Also, what about non-specific sections? How are they spread between the two (or more) files. I guess there's a reason why systemd doesn't support it.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

One use case for this would be if there were a way to plug systemd sockets into pasta, or somehow incorporate systemd-socket-proxyd into the container's networking stack such that you could pass a socket in for services that don't natively support it. I can see that being a lot of implementation work though
